danger_signals.visual_form: Animated sprite enemies (frogs, spiders, other creatures)
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile entity that causes life loss on contact

danger_signals.visual_form: Pit obstacles, cave walls, water sections

danger_signals.meaning: Story-critical danger state requiring resource expenditure to resolve

safety_signals.meaning: Extended player resilience through score milestone achievement

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Starting position in cave entrance; returns to starting point as win condition

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Unused spider sprites suggest PC-88 version had different enemy designs

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Located deep in caverns; require item acquisition for liberation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Nonlinear cave maze with multiple branching paths; inventory of collected items visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Harry navigates freely, enemies patrol predetermined patterns, items remain in fixed locations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rhonda appears as stone statue sprite
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cannot complete rescue until specific liberation items acquired

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Quickclaw visible but inaccessible, visually constrained in cage/location
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Requires item acquisition before interaction succeeds
Return-to-start becomes viable; cave entrance becomes functional exit
Player movement locks, game state returns to title/restart

environmental_storytelling.example: Rhonda and Quickclaw positioned deep in caverns, visually separated by maze distance and petrification/imprisonment states, establishing narrative stakes
Items invisible until grabbed at specific jump moments; encourages exploration and teaches player that environment contains secrets

environmental_storytelling.visual_form: Playing card suit icons used as door keys; establishes puzzle logic and gating

environmental_storytelling.example: Nonlinear subterranean dwelling layout communicates vastness and danger through spatial complexity; Andes Mountains setting suggested through environmental design
Varied enemy types in specific cave zones establish territorial markers and environmental character

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Directional pad controls left/right movement; up arrow climbs/swims upward; jump and fire actions with A/B buttons
Sprite visual change (or animation cycle change); enemies cannot cause life loss on contact
Automated sprite animation cycles, fixed movement patterns within designated zones
Collision detection triggers life loss; visual feedback (sprite removal or damage animation)
Stationary sprite; no animation; visual state communicates unavailability until liberation items applied
Constrained animation or positioning; interaction blocked until item requirements met

notes: Super Pitfall uses minimal faction complexity; primary narrative tension stems from exploration-based resource collection rather than faction conflict. Danger and safety signals are primarily mechanical (life/invulnerability systems) rather than narrative-driven. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on spatial maze complexity and hidden object discovery. The game's 'dull' and 'repetitive' reception per sources suggests visual communication of world-state was not sufficiently varied or compelling. Unused graphics (particularly unused spider sprites) indicate original design may have included more diverse enemy visual variants. Regional differences are primarily administrative (trademark, copyright, licensing text) rather than mechanical or world-state communicative.
